coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual-emission carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of salicylic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lizhen
Chen, Meng
Yuan, Lin
Mi, Zhi
Li, Caiqing
Liu, Zhixiong
Chen, Zezhong
Wang, Ligang
Feng, Feng
Wu, Luqi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) for the sensitive detection of salicylic acid (SA) was constructed for the first time. The dual-emission CDs were synthesized by simple hydrothermal method using tartaric acid (TA) and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) as raw materials. In the presence of SA, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was enhanced at 499 nm, but remained basically unchanged at 439 nm. This phenomenon is caused by the intermolecular hydrogen bond interactions. The concentrations of SA had an excellent linear relationship with CDs' fluorescence intensity ratio (F499/F439) in a range of 1 ∼ 120 and 120 ∼ 240 μM with low detection limits of 0.68 and 1.05 μM. The established ratiometric fluorescent probe is economical, simple and green, and can be used for the effective detection of SA. In addition, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent probe was successfully used to monitor SA in facial mask and toning lotion samples with a satisfactory recovery of 99.7–106.7 %. The results show that the constructed fluorescent probe based on dual-emission CDs has a great potential for the rapid and sensitive analysis of SA in actual samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Carbon dots
Salicylic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of SA in actual sample
Detection of SA by CDs-based fluorescent probe
Detection of SA in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) particle size distribution of CDs.
(A) Full survey spectra, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum and (D) O1s spectrum of CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs solution. Inset: photograph of the CDs solution under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right).. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(A)The fluorescence spectrum of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of SA (0–240 μM). (B)and (C) are the linear relationships between F499/F439 and the concentrations of SA (1–120 and 120–240 μM). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
(A) F499 /F439 of CDs added with SA (80 μM) and various interfering substances (1 M). (B) F499 /F439 of CDs and SA system after introducing different interfering substances (1 M).
Detection of SA in real samples (n = 6).

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual-emission carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of salicylic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Institute of Energy, Jinzhong 030600, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Institute of Energy, Jinzhong 030600, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Quanzhou Normal University, Quanzhou 362000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Quanzhou Normal University, Quanzhou 362000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China (Z. Mi, F. Feng).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of dual-emission CDs and fluorescence detection of SA.

In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) for the sensitive detection of salicylic acid (SA) was constructed for the first time. The dual-emission CDs were synthesized by simple hydrothermal method using tartaric acid (TA) and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) as raw materials. In the presence of SA, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was enhanced at 499 nm, but remained basically unchanged at 439 nm. This phenomenon is caused by the intermolecular hydrogen bond interactions. The concentrations of SA had an excellent linear relationship with CDs' fluorescence intensity ratio (F499/F439) in a range of 1 ∼ 120 and 120 ∼ 240 μM with low detection limits of 0.68 and 1.05 μM. The established ratiometric fluorescent probe is economical, simple and green, and can be used for the effective detection of SA. In addition, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent probe was successfully used to monitor SA in facial mask and toning lotion samples with a satisfactory recovery of 99.7–106.7 %. The results show that the constructed fluorescent probe based on dual-emission CDs has a great potential for the rapid and sensitive analysis of SA in actual samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, cosmetics are widely used in people's daily life to clean the skin and treat skin diseases [1]. Salicylic acid (SA, Fig. S1) is an important material with antibacterial, bactericidal, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory effects [2]. SA is usually added to cosmetics as a preservative [3,4]. However, overuse of SA can cause harm to the body, such as decreased defense and leading to allergies [5]. Therefore, it is of great significance for the analysis of SA content in cosmetics. So far, many analytical techniques for SA detection have been known, such as fluorescence spectrophotometry [6], colorimetry [7,8], electrochemistry [9–12], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [13] and sequential injection chromatography [14] method. Although the above methods can be used for routine analysis of SA, most of them are complex to operate, require expensive instruments and long analysis times. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a simple, convenient, economical, rapid and sensitive method to monitor SA content in real samples.

As a kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) are favored by people because of their simple synthesis process, low toxicity, excellent water solubility and great optical stability [15–19]. Fluorescent probes based on CDs for detecting small molecules and metal ions have been widely reported [19–23]. However, most of these probes are constructed from single-emission fluorescent CDs. Fluorescent probe with single response signal is easily affected by light source intensity, probe concentration and environmental factors, resulting in a large error in the analysis results [24,25]. The ratiometric fluorescent probe with double emission response signal can overcome the disadvantage of single emission fluorescence probe. For a long time, researchers have been committed to developing ratiometric fluorescent probes with dual emission signals [26–29]. Up to now, the ratiometric fluorescent probes based on dual-emission CDs have been used to detect many compounds [30,31], but the detection of SA has not been reported.

Herein, for the first time, a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual-emission CDs was designed for the sensitive determination of SA. The CDs were prepared with tartaric acid (TA) and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared CDs has good stability and excellent water solubility. There are two fluorescence emission peaks at 499 nm and 439 nm for CDs at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. After adding SA, the fluorescence at 499 nm is enhanced, while the fluorescence at 439 nm is basically unchanged. Based on this, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe was established for the highly sensitive detection of SA. The proposed fluorescent probe has the advantages of simplicity, high sensitivity, economic, wide linear range, high speed and good selectivity. Finally, the ratiometric fluorescence probe based on dual-emission CDs was applied to the analysis of SA in real samples and satisfactory results were obtained.

Salicylic acid (SA), m-phenylenediamine (mPD), NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, BaCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, CuCl2, PbCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) were received from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Tartaric acid (TA), potassium sorbate (PS), sodium benzoate (SB), sodium diacetate (SD), ammonium dodecyl sulfate (ADS), ethylhexylglycerin (EHG), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) were got from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The phosphate buffer solutions were prepared by mixing a solution of 10 mM NaH2PO4 and 10 mM Na2HPO4 and adjusting the different pH values with 1 M NaOH or 1 M HCl. All the above chemicals can be directly used without any treatment. Deionized water (18.25 MΩ) was applied during all works.

The CDs were synthesized by one- step hydrothermal method. Firstly, mPD (0.06 g), TA (0.06 g), and deionized water (20 mL) were put into the beaker and completely mixed. Secondly, the uniformly mixed solution was put into the teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted for 12 h at 180 ℃. After the reaction finished, the crude product was filtered through the filter membrane (0.22 µm), and then dialyzed in deionized water with a Spectrum Laboratories cellulose ester dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 48 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain the CDs powder and stored in a refrigerator (4 ℃) for future use.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out on an F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). The Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA) was used to obtain the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was obtained by Lambda 35 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was completed using Tecnai G2 F20 high-resolution TEM (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was provided by K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA). Luminescence lifetimes were measured using a QM8000 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan).

SA was detected in phosphate buffer solution at room temperature. Normally, 10 μL of CDs solution (0.25 mg/mL) was mixed with different volumes of SA solution and diluted to 3 mL by adding phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7). The fluorescence spectrum of the mixed solution was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm after 10 min incubation. The emission slit and excitation slit are both 5 nm. The CDs' fluorescence intensity ratio (F499/F439) was calculated as a response signal. Different metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+ and Mg2+) and potential coexisting substances (PS, SB, SD, ADS, EHG and EDTA-2Na) were selected for the anti-interference and selectivity experiments, and the test procedure was similar to the above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SA in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of probe to detect SA was studied by using facial mask and toning lotion as test samples. Facial mask and toning lotion were purchased from local supermarkets. For facial mask, 5 mL facial mask solution was accurately measured and diluted to 10 mL with deionized water. The diluted facial mask solution was filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm). The filtrate was collected and stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for further analysis. For toning lotion, 20 mL toning lotion was accurately measured and filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm). The filtrate was collected and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. For the detection, 10 μL of CDs solution (0.25 mg/mL) was mixed with 1 mL sample filtrate and diluted to 3 mL by adding phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7). Then, the F499/F439 of CDs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm after 10 min incubation. The appropriate amount of SA solution was added into the filtrate of the facial mask and toning lotion as the spiked sample for recovery analysis.

The size and morphology of the CDs were characterized by TEM. The CDs are spherical and have good dispersion (Fig. 1 A). The size distribution of CDs is shown in Fig. 1B. The particle size of CDs is 11–32 nm with an average size of 18 nm by randomly counting 200 CDs particles.

The surface structure and elemental composition of prepared CDs were analyzed by XPS and FTIR. The peaks appearing at 3439, 3252, 3082, 1664, 1616, 1484, 1307 and 1157 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2) represent the stretching vibration of O–H, N–H, C–H, C = O, C = C, C = N, C-N and C-O group, respectively [32,33]. The peak at 760 cm−1 assigned to the bending vibration of N–H group. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2 A) shows that there are three main peaks at 285.08, 399.08 and 532.08 eV, representing the existence of C (72.48 %), O (20.44 %) and N (7.08 %), respectively. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B), three peaks are divided as C–C/C = C (284.78 eV), C-N (285.98 eV) and C = O (288.88 eV) [34]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) contains two peaks of N–H (401.48 eV) and C-N (399.88 eV) [35]. Three peaks of C = O (531.18 eV), C-O (532.08 eV) and O–H (533.78 eV) were observed in O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) [36,37]. XPS analysis showed that carbonyl, amino, and hydroxyl group were attached to the surface of the prepared CDs, which made them have good solubility in water. The results were consistent with the analysis of the FTIR spectra.

The optical properties of CDs were characterized by UV and fluorescence spectra. In the UV spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 A), the peak at 287 nm corresponds to the n–π* transition (C = N/C = O), and at 233 nm belongs to the π–π* transition (C = C) [38,39]. At the optimal excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths are 439 and 499 nm, respectively. The CDs are displayed as green fluorescence under an UV lamp (λ = 365 nm), while under the sunlight, it is light yellow (Inset of Fig. 3A). The fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3B) shows that CDs has two emission peaks. It can be seen from previous reports that the fluorescence of dual-emission CDs may originate from carbon source core and surface molecular fluorescence groups [40,41]. The short wavelength emission band corresponds to the carbon core state emission, while the long wavelength emission band is the surface molecular state emission [41–43]. When the excitation wavelength increases from 350 nm to 400 nm, both emission peaks are red shifted. The origin of this red shifted phenomenon is caused by multiple emission sites on the surface and different particle sizes [16]. It is obvious that the excitation wavelength has an effect on the fluorescence intensity of both emission peaks. In order to obtain the appropriate fluorescence intensity ratio of the two emission peaks and improve the detection sensitivity, 380 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength in the following experiment. The absolute quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated to be 14.4 %. In addition, the stability of CDs was examined. As shown in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained basically unchanged in different concentrations of NaCl solutions (0–200 mM), indicating that the CDs has excellent salt tolerance. As shown in Fig. S3B, the fluorescence intensity of CDs basically does not change under continuous Xe lamp irradiation for 120 min, indicating that CDs has excellent photobleaching resistance. Therefore, the prepared CDs has good stability, which ensures its potential practical application value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SA by CDs-based fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore better measuring conditions, the influences of pH and incubation time on F499/F439 of CDs and SA system were examined. The value of F499/F439 at different pH (4–9) are shown in Fig. S4 A. F499/F439 rises slowly in the pH range of 4–7, then drops rapidly in the pH range of 7–9, and reaches the maximum value at pH 7. Hence, the subsequent experiments were carried out at pH 7. Fig. S4B shows the change of F499/F439 within 0–60 min. F499/F439 tends to be stable after 10 min of SA addition. Therefore, 10 min was selected as the best incubation time. Under the optimal conditions, the relationship between F499/F439 and SA concentration was studied. As shown in Fig. 4A, with the addition of SA, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increases at 499 nm, while the fluorescence intensity remains basically unchanged at 439 nm, indicating that SA can selectively enhance the fluorescence of CDs, which makes it possible to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe. As can be seen from Fig. 4B and C, F499/F439 has a good linear relationship with SA concentration in the range of 1–120 and 120–240 μM. The linear equations are F499/F439 = 0.004[C] + 0.93 (R2 = 0.9956) and F499/F439 = 0.0026[C] + 1.1291 (R2 = 0.9966), respectively. The corresponding limits of detection (LODs) are 0.68 and 1.05 μM, respectively (LOD = 3σ/k, σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and k is the slope of the correction curve). Compared with the previously reported methods for the determination of SA (Table S1) [44–49], our developed method has a lower LOD than other reported methods, although it is inferior to the mass spectrometry detection method. However, mass spectrometry requires expensive instruments, time-consuming sample preparation, and complex operations. More importantly, the proposed CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe can effectively eliminate background signals and environmental interference, and is convenient, easy to operate, cheap, fast, and has good sensitive, which is suitable for the routine detection of SA.

In order to understand the mechanism of CDs fluorescence enhancing by SA, the UV absorption of SA and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs were carried out and displayed in Fig. S5 A. There is no spectral overlap between the UV absorption of SA and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs. This indicates that there is no energy transfer process between CD and SA [50]. The fluorescence enhancement mechanism is probably caused by intermolecular hydrogen bonds between CDs and SA [51]. It can be seen from the IR and XPS results that the surface of CDs contains a large amount of –NH2, –OH and –COOH group. SA contains –OH and –COOH group, which can easily form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds between CDs and analyte SA. As shown in Fig. 4A, the CDs exhibit two fluorescence emission peaks at 439 and 499 nm. The fluorescence emission peaks at 439 and 499 nm correspond to carbon core state emission and surface molecular state emission of CDs, respectively [41–43]. The formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds can effectively limit the vibration and rotation of functional groups on the surface of CDs, reduce non radiative decay processes, and thus increase the fluorescence intensity at 499 nm, while the fluorescence intensity at 439 nm remains basically unchanged [52–54]. To confirm the fluorescence enhancement of CDs caused by intermolecular hydrogen bonds, the FTIR spectrum of CDs with and without of SA were investigated and illustrated in Fig. S5B. the C-OH vibration of CDs was 3373 and 3451 cm−1 with and without of SA, respectively. The C-OH vibration of CDs with SA is blue-shifted by 78 cm−1 compared to CDs without SA. This blue shift indicates the presence of hydrogen bonds between CDs and SA [55]. It is further proved that the intermolecular hydrogen bond interactions lead to enhanced fluorescence of CDs.

In order to study the selectivity of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probe, the common metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+ and Mg2+) and potential coexisting substances (PS, SB, SD, ADS, EHG and EDTA-2Na) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were evaluated. Fig. 5A shows F499/F439 of CDs added with various interfering substances. It is obvious that the common metal ions and potential coexisting substances have little influence on F499/F439 except for SA, indicating that the CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probe has high selectivity for SA. At the same time, the anti-interference performance was evaluated. Various interfering substances were added to CDs solution containing SA, and the F499/F439 of CDs was recorded. As shown in Fig. 5B, the introduction of interfering substances has little effect on the measurement of SA, indicating that the established CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probe has excellent anti-interference ability to SA. In conclusion, the proposed CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe has good selectivity and anti-interference ability, which can be effectively used for the detection of SA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SA in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for real samples analysis was investigated by detecting of SA content in facial mask and toning lotion samples. The recovery test was performed using the standard addition method. The results are displayed in Table 1 , the recovery was 99.7–106.7 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 6) was 0.7–1.5 %. The analysis results show that the proposed CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe has good accuracy and precision, and can be used as an effective platform for SA detection in actual samples.

In conclusion, a dual-emission fluorescent CDs was prepared from TA and mPD. The CDs has good water solubility and excellent stability. The synthesized CDs can be used as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the sensitive detection of SA, with a wide linear range (1–240 μM) and a lower LOD (0.68 μM). It is worth noting that the CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe used to detect SA has not yet been reported. In addition, the proposed CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe has the advantages of simplicity, convenience, rapidity, sensitivity and selectivity. It has been successfully used for the effective detection of SA in actual sample, with recovery of 99.7–106.7 % and RSD of 0.7–1.5 %. The results show that the established CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe has potential value and practical application prospect in the detection of SA.

Lizhen Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Meng Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Validation. Lin Yuan: Investigation, Methodology. Zhi Mi: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Caiqing Li: Methodology. Zhixiong Liu: Data curation. Zezhong Chen: Software, Methodology. Ligang Wang: Software, Data curation. Feng Feng: Conceptualization, Supervision. Luqi Wu: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (202303021211329), the Higher Education Technology Innovation Projects of Shanxi Province (2021L375 and 2021L387) and the Doctoral Science Research Funds of Shanxi Datong University (2016-B-13 and 2014-B-13).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123232.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
